That's how I got to, you know, and I'm still asking a lot of whys, but I got to a really good point, at least on some pieces of the plastic issue that we have.
turns.zh: 我以前对塑料的最终归宿知之甚少。我当时想，印尼到处是垃圾，那为什么不像美国那样做呢？直接回收不就行了？只要做好分类收集就行。然后你开始深入挖掘，开始学习。你试着建模，觉得照搬美国的做法就能解决问题。接着你开始问更多问题，比如，这些垃圾到底去了哪里？你越挖越深。我常说，连续问五次为什么，通常就能得到很好的答案或真正关键的问题。为什么这样？为什么那样？为什么这样？所以对我来说，研究塑料的过程就是一连串的为什么，你不停地追问，从不轻信任何人的表面答案。这完全是一个研究的过程。直到有一天我突然意识到，等等，这套逻辑在这里根本不成立。等等，有人给我们编了个故事，说塑料在生命周期结束时是如何处理的，但现实根本不是那样。真正发生了什么？你越挖越深。所以对我来说，关键就是：你开始应用这些知识，永不放弃，直到找到真正的根本原因，然后从根本上解决问题，而不是修修补补。我就是这样一步步走到今天的，而且我还在问很多为什么，但至少对塑料问题的某些方面，我已经有了相当深刻的认识。

transcript.sec: Two Types of Plastics: Durable vs. Ephemeral

turns.en: Can you talk about where you are now in your thinking?
turns.zh: 能谈谈你现在的想法到了什么阶段吗？

turns.en: Absolutely. So it first started with collection and waste management: let's just collect it all and make sure it doesn't get there, and then we'll recycle it. But what I'm mostly talking about now is there are two types of plastics. There are plastics that are durable, things like your car bumpers. They last for five or ten years, they serve a purpose, and the material is really good for that. Okay. Then there's another purpose, which we call ephemeral or single-use or very low use plastics, where we're using this product once or a couple of times and then we discard it. There's still a lot of things that need to be done on the durable side, and it's not perfect by any means. But if you look at what's growing exponentially, it's packaging and single-use and these ephemeral applications that are going wild. In fact, many of the petrochemical companies, like the petroleum companies, they're actually not worried about electric vehicles taking away all the gas because they see these petrochemicals, all these plastics and these things consuming oil over time. So you're like, wait a second, the petrochemical guys aren't scared because we're using so much plastic in packaging and it's growing crazy.
turns.zh: 当然。最开始是收集和废物管理：我们把所有垃圾都收集起来，确保它们不会乱丢，然后进行回收。但现在我主要谈的是两种塑料。一种是耐用塑料，比如汽车保险杠，它们能用五到十年，有实际用途，材料也很适合。另一种我们称之为短暂塑料、一次性塑料或极低使用频率塑料，这类产品只用一两次就被丢弃了。耐用塑料方面还有很多工作要做，远非完美。但看看增长最快的领域，包装、一次性用品和这些短暂应用正在疯狂扩张。事实上，很多石化公司，也就是石油公司，他们根本不担心电动汽车会取代汽油，因为他们看到这些石化产品——所有塑料制品——正在持续消耗石油。所以你会想，等等，石化巨头们并不害怕，因为我们在包装上用了这么多塑料，而且用量还在疯狂增长。

turns.en: So is it really recycling?
turns.zh: 那么，回收真的有效吗？

turns.en: So I'm talking about packaging and recycling. I started recycling at the University of Michigan back in the 80s. When you start to learn about it, you find out that just because it has the recyclable label on it, it's not recycled. It's literally buried or burned, setting off toxic ashes. Literally, it's buried or burned, or it ends up in the environment like we see as litter or in the oceans. You're like, wait a second, just because it has a recycling label doesn't mean it's recycled. Very, very few of them are actually recycled. And you're like, okay, this is a problem, and it's growing exponentially. Even the best countries in the world with all these resources cannot afford to recycle because it doesn't make any economic sense. So I'm like, wait a second, what happens to all this stuff once it's burned or buried? Then you find out it turns into micro and nano plastics that remain in the environment for 500 years. Then you learn it's in everything we drink, it's in the air we breathe, it's in the soil, it's in the water, it's in snow. It's falling down as rain or snow on the polar caps. You're like, wait a second, this plastic is everywhere. It's going to be everywhere for 500 years. And it's these tiny nano plastics. Just because it goes away, it's still there. And you're like, well, what does that do? It goes into our body. Typically, those nano plastics pick up bio-accumulative toxins, and those toxins literally latch onto them, and then they go into our bodies and into our blood streams. We have very, very early research—it isn't published yet—but I've talked to a lot of researchers. When those nano plastics get into us, it's like asbestos. It causes inflammation in the body, and the body attacks it, and it could cause all kinds of things. We still have to do the correlation. So people say, oh, it's nano plastics, don't worry, it's in your water but it's small parts per million. But look, I think it's just a lot of people telling us these things don't matter, just like they said cigarettes don't matter, or sugar doesn't matter. It matters. And it's in us, it's in our environment, and we're polluting for generations.
turns.zh: 我谈的是包装和回收问题。我在 80 年代就在密歇根大学开始做回收了。当你深入了解后会发现，就算产品上有可回收标志，它也不一定真的被回收了。实际上，它们要么被填埋，要么被焚烧，产生有毒灰烬。真的就是埋掉或烧掉，或者直接进入环境，成为我们看到的垃圾或海洋污染物。你会想，等等，有回收标志不代表它真的被回收了。真正被回收的少之又少。然后你意识到，这是个问题，而且还在指数级增长。即使是世界上资源最丰富的国家，也负担不起回收的成本，因为经济上根本不可行。所以我想，等等，这些东西被焚烧或填埋后会发生什么？然后你发现它们会变成微塑料和纳米塑料，在环境中存留 500 年。接着你发现，它们存在于我们喝的水、呼吸的空气、土壤、水、雪中，甚至以雨雪的形式降落在极地冰盖上。你会想，等等，这种塑料无处不在，而且将持续存在 500 年。这些微小的纳米塑料，虽然看不见了，但依然存在。那么，它们会带来什么影响呢？它们会进入我们的身体。通常，这些纳米塑料会吸附生物累积性毒素，这些毒素会牢牢附着在塑料上，然后进入我们的身体和血液。目前有非常初步的研究——尚未发表——但我与许多研究人员交流过。当这些纳米塑料进入人体后，就像石棉一样，会引起体内炎症，身体会攻击它们，可能导致各种问题。我们还需要进行相关性研究。所以有人说，哦，纳米塑料没关系，水里只有百万分之几。但我觉得，很多人都在告诉我们这些东西不重要，就像他们曾经说香烟不重要、糖不重要一样。但事实是，它们很重要。它们已经存在于我们体内，存在于环境中，我们正在污染子孙后代的环境。

transcript.sec: The Solution: New Materials and Design

turns.en: So okay, now we have this nano plastics problem. It's gonna be there for 500 years. Packaging's going crazy. It can't be recycled. So what do we do? We have to come up with new materials and new designs for all this packaging to allow us to live like they do. This is a great thing in Southeast Asia: when you order something, we get takeout or you get anything, it comes out of banana leaf or it's wrapped in a banana leaf. You're like, oh cool. And you take the banana leaf, you could just chuck it. Obviously you put it in a compost bin, but if you don't and it just ends up on the street, it goes away in 30, 40, 50 days. So we need to get to that type of packaging. So guess what? There are packages like that out there that are cost effective, from different materials. Some of it's called PHA. There are other ones out there that can come from waste product, by waste to create this, whether it's a film, a bottle cap, a bottle, these different things that can then be consumer compostable. So literally, you can just drop it in. If you litter it, it goes away. If you put it in the ocean, it goes away in 45 to 120 days. No nano plastics, no micro plastics. Just goes away. And that is the holy grail. There are companies that are doing that today, and we're trying to find them and help them, and try to get people to design with them. That's what we need to do. In the meantime, we need to stop all this single-use stuff. We need to literally ban it, because we are creating a huge mess on this planet, and we just don't realize it.
turns.zh: 那么，现在我们有了纳米塑料问题，它将持续 500 年。包装用量疯狂增长，又无法回收。我们该怎么办？我们必须为所有包装开发新材料和新设计，让包装像自然界那样循环。东南亚有个很好的例子：当你点外卖或买东西时，食物是用香蕉叶包裹的。你会觉得，哇，真酷。然后你把香蕉叶扔掉——当然最好是放进堆肥箱——但就算丢在街上，它也会在 30 到 50 天内自然降解。我们需要这种类型的包装。事实上，现在已经存在这样的包装，成本效益高，由不同材料制成。其中一种叫 PHA。还有其他材料，可以利用废弃物来制造，无论是薄膜、瓶盖、瓶子，这些都可以做到消费后可堆肥。也就是说，你可以直接丢弃。就算乱扔，它也会降解；如果掉进海里，45 到 120 天内就会消失，不会产生纳米塑料或微塑料。这就是终极解决方案。现在已经有公司在做这件事了，我们正在寻找并帮助他们，同时推动人们用这些材料进行设计。这就是我们需要做的。与此同时，我们必须停止使用所有一次性塑料制品，甚至应该直接禁止，因为我们正在给地球制造巨大的混乱，而人们还没有意识到这一点。

transcript.secZh: 解决方案：新材料与设计

turns.en: I would add one more thing. You can add three if you'd like. Recycling: just because it's recyclable doesn't mean it's recycled. So don't trust your recycling labels. If you do recycle, you should still recycle, but for the most part, it's probably not going anywhere to be reused. Recycled plastics, even if they are recycled, are only good for two to three uses, and then they become bad plastics because they're mechanically recycled. The long chains of plastic become short chains, and they can't make things with them, and they become too brittle. So literally, you can only recycle them a few times, unlike aluminum, glass, and paper. The next thing is that people say, "Oh, if you don't recycle, you can compost it." Maybe you saw these boxed water things, you know, like boxed water. No, it's right. They say you can recycle it, but you have to go to a special facility in the middle of nowhere. It doesn't really get recycled. You hear things about compostable things. What that means is it has to go into an industrial composting facility and get special heat treatment and air treatment and all this stuff to be able to break down. That doesn't work either. So we have been greenwashing recycling, compostable, all of these different languages used to say plastics are green or they're bio-based. Wrong, wrong, wrong. The only thing that really matters now is bio-waste-based plastics that are consumer compostable. That's the only thing that we should be selecting. And if we can't select that, pick a different material. Do not use plastics. I know that was a diatribe, long-winded, but I'm trying to get that message out to people. That's why it's really important. That's why we have long-form conversations, so we can unpack some of this stuff, so to speak.
turns.zh: 我想再补充一点。你可以加三点，如果你愿意的话。回收：可回收并不意味着它真的被回收了。所以不要相信你的回收标签。如果你确实回收了，你仍然应该回收，但大多数情况下，它可能不会被重新利用。回收的塑料，即使被回收了，也只能用两到三次，然后它们就变成了劣质塑料，因为它们是机械回收的。塑料的长链变成了短链，无法用来制造东西，而且变得非常脆。所以实际上，你只能回收它们几次，不像铝、玻璃和纸。下一件事是，人们说，“哦，如果你不回收，你可以堆肥。”也许你见过这些盒装水的东西，你知道，就像盒装水。不，没错。他们说你可以回收它，但你必须去一个偏僻的特殊设施。它实际上并没有被回收。你听说过可堆肥的东西。那意味着它必须进入工业堆肥设施，并经过特殊的热处理和空气处理才能分解。那也行不通。所以我们一直在漂绿回收、可堆肥，所有这些不同的说法都是为了说塑料是绿色的或生物基的。错，错，错。现在唯一真正重要的是基于生物废物的、消费者可堆肥的塑料。那是我们唯一应该选择的。如果我们不能选择那个，就选一种不同的材料。不要使用塑料。我知道这是一段长篇大论，但我想把这条信息传达给人们。这就是为什么它非常重要。这就是为什么我们有长对话，这样我们可以深入探讨这些东西。

turns.en: You mentioned finding companies, helping companies. Is that done through Future Shape or through a different vehicle? And for people who don't know Future Shape, could you describe what it is?
turns.zh: 你提到了寻找公司、帮助公司。那是通过 Future Shape 还是通过其他方式？对于不了解 Future Shape 的人，你能描述一下它是什么吗？

turns.en: Future Shape is an investment vehicle from our family to find great entrepreneurs and companies trying to do really hard developments and create deep technologies to help fix our planet, fix our societies, fix our communities, and get us forward in a green way, in some way green, or to enable small and medium business owners to then be able to flourish and get out from underneath the system in some way that they've been subject to for a long time. So we try to find these things, and we don't call ourselves venture capitalists, we don't call ourselves angels. We call ourselves mentors with money. We are mentors first, and we come and we put our money where our mouth is to help these entrepreneurs, to help these companies realize these goals, these dreams, these technologies to help our world. And they take time to build, right? They take time, effort, and belief in what's going on. So that's what we do. We've invested in over 200 companies around the world, and we mentor them at certain parts of their lifecycle, and just try to be that team that can help them with the confidence when they're down, when they're doing the right thing, or help them find whatever resources they need, or help them when they're trying to learn leadership, you know, how to do leadership, or find the right person, or do the hiring. All those kinds of real things that if you haven't done them for a long time, you may not feel confident about. So it's that nudge to help them. Maybe we act like big brother or big sister sometimes.
turns.zh: Future Shape 是我们家族的一个投资工具，旨在寻找伟大的创业者和公司，他们正在努力进行非常艰难的开发，创造深层技术，以帮助修复我们的星球、修复我们的社会、修复我们的社区，并以某种绿色方式推动我们前进，或者使中小型企业主能够蓬勃发展，并以某种方式摆脱他们长期受制于的系统。所以我们试图找到这些东西，我们不称自己为风险资本家，也不称自己为天使投资人。我们称自己为“有资金的导师”。我们首先是导师，然后我们言行一致，投入资金来帮助这些创业者，帮助这些公司实现这些目标、这些梦想、这些技术，以帮助我们的世界。而这些都需要时间来构建，对吧？它们需要时间、努力和对所发生事情的信念。这就是我们所做的。我们投资了全球超过 200 家公司，并在它们生命周期的某些阶段提供指导，努力成为那个团队，在他们低落时、在做正确的事情时给予信心，或帮助他们找到所需的资源，或帮助他们学习领导力，你知道，如何做领导，或找到合适的人，或进行招聘。所有这些真实的事情，如果你很长时间没有做过，你可能不会感到自信。所以这是一种推动来帮助他们。有时我们可能像大哥或大姐一样。

turns.en: Is Future Shape where you're putting most of your energy these days, or what are you personally spending most of your energy on?
turns.zh: Future Shape 是你现在投入大部分精力的地方吗？还是你个人主要把精力花在什么地方？

turns.en: Future Shape has been going now for about eight years, maybe ten, depending on how you count. And I spent almost all of my time on Future Shape and my team. Our team together, we all work on these companies because what we've learned, I learned over the time, is we could do one thing and start another company, but we take all of the experience, the knowledge, the resources that we can bring to bear anywhere in the world and help these other 200 companies to thrive and really make the change that they want to make in this world. So we want to use a leverage model. We're really trying to leverage it, and for us it's incredibly fun, and our curiosity is piqued all the time when they're going after tough problems. And we get to dive in and be beginners with them in a way, or get to learn from them, and then we get to take our expertise and combine it with their expertise. So it's incredibly rewarding. And if people didn't mentor me when I was growing up and going through that thing, I would never be here today. So at some point I looked around and went, "Wait a second, I guess that's me now." It's time to try to live up to what the mentors taught me, try to live to that ideal, and pass the baton now, and instill this art, this craft with these other people that we try to help. Because it's really important that we try to pass the stuff down to try to make it better for other generations. It's important work.
turns.zh: Future Shape 已经运作了大约八年，也许十年，取决于你怎么算。我几乎把所有时间都花在 Future Shape 和我的团队上。我们的团队一起，我们都为这些公司工作，因为我们学到的是，我们可以做一件事并创办另一家公司，但我们把所有的经验、知识、资源带到世界各地，帮助其他 200 家公司蓬勃发展，真正实现他们想要在这个世界上做出的改变。所以我们想使用一个杠杆模型。我们真的在努力利用它，对我们来说这非常有趣，当他们追求棘手的问题时，我们的好奇心总是被激发。我们得以深入其中，在某种程度上和他们一起成为初学者，或者向他们学习，然后我们运用我们的专业知识，与他们的专业知识相结合。所以这非常有益。如果在我成长和经历那些事情时没有人指导我，我今天就不会在这里。所以在某个时刻，我环顾四周，心想，“等等，现在轮到我了。”是时候努力不辜负导师们教给我的东西，努力达到那个理想，现在传递接力棒，并将这门艺术、这门手艺灌输给我们试图帮助的其他人。因为把东西传递下去，让后代变得更好，这非常重要。这是重要的工作。

transcript.sec: Being a Beginner and Continuous Learning

turns.en: You mentioned being beginners, and that's something I came up in your TED talk within the context of Steve and looking at things with beginners' eyes. Are there any particular skills that you're currently focused on personally, or new behaviors, either ending or starting behaviors within the personal realm? Or I should say individual, probably a better way to put it. Is there anything that you're currently working on?
turns.zh: 你提到了成为初学者，这是我在你的 TED 演讲中看到的，在史蒂夫的背景下，用初学者的眼光看事物。你个人目前有没有特别关注的技能，或者新的行为，无论是结束还是开始的行为，在个人领域？或者我应该说个体，可能更好的说法。你目前有什么在努力的事情吗？

turns.en: From a beginner standpoint, in many ways I'm in the plastics right now. I'm just a little guy calling up researchers saying, "Could you help me? I'm trying to get this. Well, how? Why does it work this way?" You know, reading research papers and really going back to school, being an undergrad in a way, and learning hopefully from the best. So I try to put myself in a position where I'm incredibly curious. I have skills, analytical skills to understand maybe what they're doing, but I don't really have the data to really understand it to that level. So for me, I'm always trying to put myself in an uncomfortable position to make big decisions, like should we invest, should we do whatever, because that kind of tension makes things really fun and really exciting for me. I call it brain food. So I try to do that every day with the companies. We help to invest in Impossible Foods. I didn't know anything about food science, know anything about that, so go learn about that. Or we're doing Modern Meadow, which is leather without a cow, right? How do you grow skin without an organism, without a larger organism? What does that mean? How? Whatever. So all of these things.
turns.zh: 从初学者的角度来看，在很多方面我现在就在塑料领域。我只是一个小人物，打电话给研究人员说，“你能帮我吗？我想弄明白这个。嗯，怎么做到的？为什么它会这样工作？”你知道，阅读研究论文，真正回到学校，在某种程度上像个本科生，希望向最优秀的人学习。所以我努力让自己处于极度好奇的位置。我有技能，分析技能，也许能理解他们在做什么，但我没有足够的数据真正理解到那个水平。所以对我来说，我总是努力让自己处于不舒服的位置来做重大决定，比如我们是否应该投资，是否应该做任何事，因为那种紧张感让事情对我来说非常有趣和令人兴奋。我称之为“大脑食物”。所以我每天都和公司一起这样做。我们帮助投资了 Impossible Foods。我对食品科学一无所知，对那方面一无所知，所以去学习它。或者我们做 Modern Meadow，那是没有牛的皮革，对吧？你怎么在没有有机体的情况下培养皮肤，没有更大的有机体？那意味着什么？怎么做？等等。所有这些东西。

turns.en: For people who are listening or watching, by any objective measure, people would consider you successful. And what I like to do in these conversations, and fortunately in your bio it's very clear that it wasn't a linear path from bottom left to top right. You followed a very jagged path. What do you struggle with? What are things that you find difficult?
turns.zh: 对于正在收听或观看的人来说，按任何客观标准，人们都会认为你很成功。在这些对话中我喜欢做的，而且幸运的是在你的履历中也很清楚，那并不是一条从左下到右上的线性路径。你走的是一条非常曲折的路。你挣扎的是什么？你觉得困难的事情是什么？

turns.en: For me, I think it's heavy-duty analytics. Anything that's very analytical — I have to feel it, I have to see it, I have to feel it. I can't just sit there and stare at numbers. Another thing that's really hard for me is any kind of long-form writing. That's really hard. I struggle with impatience. These things take longer than you'd like. You can see it, you can feel it, you can grasp it, but you have to go, "No, come on, let's just get there." And you have to be in the passenger seat waiting for things to happen. So I try to augment the things I don't like to do with people who like to do that. Like Mike on our team is an amazing legal guy, and this is what he loves to do. So I'm like, "Mike, just go do it. That's not my thing." But when it comes to the real emotional stuff — I want the future to be here now — that I struggle with all the time. I just don't want to wait. Why should we wait, especially when we know now is the time? There was a general magic — it wasn't the time, it was way too early. But when you see it, when you can see it perfectly, you see the moment, you're like, "Let's go, we gotta get this done, take the hill, come on." That's the most frustrating thing. There's always science fiction and future stuff, but when you see it and you can feel it, you know, go for it, let's get it done.
turns.zh: 对我来说，我觉得是重度的分析工作。任何非常分析性的事情——我得去感受它，得去看它，得去体会它。我不能只是坐在那里盯着数字。另一件对我来说非常困难的事情是任何形式的长时间写作。那真的很难。我挣扎于不耐烦。这些事情花的时间比你希望的要长。你能看到它，能感觉到它，能抓住它，但你得说，“不，快点，我们直接到那一步吧。”而你却只能坐在副驾驶座上等待事情发生。所以我试着用喜欢做那些事的人来补充我不喜欢做的事情。比如我们团队里的迈克，他是个很棒的法律专家，这就是他喜欢做的事。所以我就说，“迈克，你去做吧。那不是我的事。”但到了真正情绪化的事情上——我希望未来现在就到来——我一直都在挣扎。我就是不想等。为什么要等，尤其当我们知道现在就是时机的时候？曾经有一种普遍的魔力——那不是时机，太早了。但当你看到它，当你完美地看到它，你看到那个时刻，你就会说，“走吧，我们得搞定这个，拿下那座山头，快点。”那是最令人沮丧的事情。总是有科幻和未来的东西，但当你看到它并感觉到它时，你知道，去做吧，让我们把它完成。

turns.en: Let's dig into impatience a bit, because impatience has been — I would like to think, and maybe this is delusional — one of my greatest assets and also one of my greatest handicaps. How do you think about your impatience or work on your impatience so that you get the benefits of feeling a sense of urgency and pushing when those pushes are helpful, without being just an insufferable pain in the ass who leaves scorched earth everywhere? Because I've certainly been the latter at points where people are just like, "God damn it, doesn't happen that fast," and you're pissing everybody off. How have you thought about finding the middle path with that, if there is one?
turns.zh: 我们来深入谈谈不耐烦，因为不耐烦一直——我愿意认为，也许这是妄想——是我最大的资产之一，也是我最大的障碍之一。你是如何看待你的不耐烦，或者如何克服它的，以便你能从紧迫感和在有益时推动中获得好处，而不会成为一个到处留下焦土的令人难以忍受的讨厌鬼？因为我肯定在某些时候是后者，人们会说，“该死的，没那么快，”然后你把所有人都惹毛了。你是怎么考虑找到中间道路的，如果有的话？

turns.en: I struggle with it every day. There are times when you're working on things and working with people when you know you've been there and it should go faster. Like, "Okay, why aren't we doing this faster? Just get the stuff out the door." It's not like researching and trying to engineer and find the right correct widget for XYZ. It's really just the process of getting it done. It's like, "Come on guys, I understand you don't want to, this is a big risk and you're tentative about it, but we just got to move on, right?" So you don't want to have analysis paralysis. So you have to bucket it. You have to say, "Okay, this is just their mental state. We have to coax them through it, give them help, or help myself to get through that phase, educate, and then we got to get faster." Then there's the other phase, which is the process of discovery, the process of getting to insight. Those kinds of things always take longer. And then there's another one which is out of your control: working with other external teams that you can't really push. You just have to kind of grin and bear it, and you keep reassessing the partnership. "Should we be doing with them?" And communicating in a very positive but very direct way: "Let me tell you what we're grappling with now. It isn't all rosy. These are the kind of decisions we have to make because you are delaying, or this is happening, or this is what's happening to our business." You have to be really upfront, just like I was talking about the two managers or the two people who had to get along and figure out — we got to be upfront and honest about our partnership and how it's not working, and go, "What are we going to do to get over it?" So I think it's those types of bidding, and also just taking a step back. You tense up your neck, and you just go breathe. Make sure I get my yoga done, make sure I do what have you to say, "Okay, it's just to try to calm down in certain instances."
turns.zh: 我每天都在挣扎。有时候你在做事情，和别人一起工作，你知道你经历过，而且应该更快。比如，“好吧，为什么我们不更快地做这个？把东西弄出去就行了。”这不像是在研究和工程化，为 XYZ 找到正确的部件。这真的只是完成它的过程。就像，“拜托，伙计们，我理解你们不想做，这是个很大的风险，你们犹豫不决，但我们得继续前进，对吧？”所以你不想有分析瘫痪。所以你得把它分类。你得说，“好吧，这只是他们的心理状态。我们得哄着他们度过，给他们帮助，或者帮助我自己度过那个阶段，教育他们，然后我们得加快速度。”然后是另一个阶段，即发现的过程，获得洞察的过程。这类事情总是需要更长时间。还有另一个你无法控制的情况：与其他外部团队合作，你无法真正推动他们。你只能勉强忍受，并不断重新评估合作关系。“我们该和他们合作吗？”并以非常积极但非常直接的方式沟通：“让我告诉你我们现在在挣扎什么。一切并不都是美好的。我们必须做出这些决定，因为你正在拖延，或者发生了这个，或者这对我们的业务造成了影响。”你必须非常坦诚，就像我谈到的那两位经理或那两个人，他们必须相处并弄清楚——我们必须对我们的合作关系以及它如何不起作用坦诚相待，然后说，“我们要怎么做才能克服它？”所以我认为是这类事情，以及退一步。你脖子紧绷，然后你深呼吸。确保我做完瑜伽，确保我做些事情来说，“好吧，只是在某些情况下试着冷静下来。”

turns.en: My next question was going to be related to self-care, so the yoga is a perfect segue. What are some of your self-care practices, whether on a daily or weekly basis?
turns.zh: 我的下一个问题本来是关于自我关爱的，所以瑜伽是一个完美的过渡。你有哪些自我关爱的做法，无论是每天还是每周的？

turns.en: Well, the diet is the first one. The diet is the most important. And what is your diet? Well, it's vegetarian without caffeine without alcohol. And it's an absolute — the hardest thing in the world is refined sugars. Trying to just stay off that beast is... I like it too much. It just is what it is. So you try to do what you can, minimize that as much as possible. So that's that: eating healthy, eating whole foods, that kind of stuff. Then there's exercise. Exercising at least an hour a day, six days, maybe seven days a week. Maybe two hours or three hours, breaking it up with a hike or whatever, but always exercising. I've been doing yoga for 25 years now. That was also part of my reboot. Yoga for 25 years. I hope it will be the only exercise I will be doing when I'm 75 or older, because I think it's the right balance of mental and strength and toning and those kinds of things. What type of yoga? I'll do basically anything: Hatha, Ashtanga, Bikram, you name it. I'm not religious in that way because I always think there's more things to learn about and do, try different things, work with different instructors. Then there's weight training, and stretching, which I should be doing more of.
turns.zh: 嗯，饮食是第一位的。饮食是最重要的。你的饮食是什么样的？嗯，是素食，不含咖啡因，不含酒精。而且绝对——世界上最难的是精制糖。试图远离那个野兽……我太喜欢它了。事情就是这样。所以你尽量做你能做的，尽可能减少它。就是这样：健康饮食，吃全食物，诸如此类。然后是锻炼。每天至少锻炼一小时，每周六天，也许七天。也许两三个小时，用徒步或其他方式分开，但总是锻炼。我已经练瑜伽 25 年了。那也是我重启的一部分。瑜伽练了 25 年。我希望当我 75 岁或更老的时候，这将是我唯一会做的运动，因为我认为它是心理、力量、塑形等方面的正确平衡。什么类型的瑜伽？我基本上什么都做：哈他、阿斯汤加、比克拉姆，你说得出来。我在那方面并不教条，因为我总是认为有更多东西要学习和尝试，尝试不同的事情，与不同的教练合作。然后是举重训练和拉伸，我应该多做拉伸。

turns.en: Do you recall when? Yeah, it was around... well, it depends on when you went to sleep and when you had your last drink. But it always seemed to be about four to four and a half hours after that last drink, and you went to sleep when? At least for me, my heart would race, and then you would wake up, and then because you're just a Type A personality, you start thinking and you can't go back to sleep, and you're sweating, and then you have a horrible night's sleep. But that was it for me.
turns.zh: 你记得是什么时候吗？嗯，大概……这取决于你几点睡、最后一杯酒几点喝的。但似乎总是在最后一杯酒后大约四到四个半小时，然后你入睡。至少对我来说，心跳会加速，然后你会醒来，接着因为你是 A 型人格，开始胡思乱想，再也睡不着，还会出汗，然后整晚睡眠都很糟糕。但对我来说就是这样。

turns.en: I find it absolutely fascinating because I only grew up... so the book is all about Japan before the pre-industrial era and how they lived at home and how they lived when Western technology entered the home afterwards. And he was comparing his life before and after. And I was never able to do that because I only lived in this world, in this post-industrial world. And so he was explaining things like: we had wooden spoons before metal spoons, and we had wooden bowls before we had porcelain bowls, and just the sound of the spoon hitting the bowl when it was wood on wood versus metal on porcelain, and how that would be grating to your ear and how soothing it was when it was wood on wood. Or artificial light versus natural light and diffuse light, and how your eyes and how your mental state would be very different under these two conditions. So it was literally comparing this pre-industrial and post-industrial life through an incredible writer who could encapsulate it. And you start to go, 'Wait a second, I never considered those details in my life to this level.' And you're like, 'That's about staying beginner, right?' You say, 'Yeah, oh my god, I've never even thought about that.' And this makes me rethink so many things that I never thought of, and it takes you to a whole other world. Yet the one that stuck with me most concretely was the lacquerware. I lived in Japan for a year in high school as an exchange student and lived with three families, so I got to see different rooms in the house and different styles of home. And there's a short portion in 'In Praise of Shadows' that talks about how gaudy certain types of... you could consider them sort of Chinese-influenced Japanese bowls and furniture are because they're covered in gold plating or painted to look very bright and gold lacquered. Exactly. And what was one of my gifts to myself after finishing my second book, which is definitely not sure... well, it is more of a homework assignment, was a lacquered Japanese saddle actually, which kind of typifies this. And the point in the book was that it looks gaudy because things were lacquered in those types of materials due to dim lighting. They were intended to be looked at in very dim lighting, and as soon as you put incandescent modern lighting on it, it looks terrible. And I was like, 'Wow, I've never even considered the source and strength of lighting.' And then it just totally changes how you look around at different objects, different historical objects, right? You kind of take as a given that the lighting is a constant, but of course it's not a constant in retrospect.
turns.zh: 我觉得它绝对迷人，因为我只经历过……这本书讲的是日本前工业时代，人们在家如何生活，以及西方技术后来进入家庭后他们如何生活。作者对比了之前和之后的生活。而我永远无法做到这一点，因为我只活在这个后工业世界。所以他解释了一些事情：比如我们以前用木勺，后来用金属勺；以前用木碗，后来用瓷碗。木勺碰木碗的声音与金属碰瓷器的声音，前者多么悦耳，后者多么刺耳。还有人造光与自然光、漫射光，你的眼睛和精神状态在这两种条件下会截然不同。所以这简直是通过一位了不起的作家，浓缩地比较了前工业和后工业生活。你会开始想：“等等，我从未在生命中如此细致地考虑过这些细节。”然后你会说：“这就是保持初学者心态，对吧？”你会说：“天哪，我从未想过这个。”这让我重新思考许多从未想过的事情，把你带入一个完全不同的世界。但最具体让我印象深刻的是漆器。我高中时在日本做了一年交换生，住在三个家庭里，所以看到了不同的房间和家居风格。《阴翳礼赞》中有一段谈到某些……你可以认为是受中国影响的日本碗和家具多么俗艳，因为它们镀金或涂成亮色，金漆。没错。我完成第二本书后（那本书更像一份作业），送给自己的礼物之一就是一个日本漆器马鞍，它正好体现了这一点。书中的观点是，这些东西看起来俗艳，是因为它们原本是在昏暗灯光下欣赏的。一旦你用现代白炽灯照射，就变得很难看。我当时想：“哇，我从未考虑过光源和强度。”这完全改变了你观察不同物品、不同历史物品的方式，对吧？你理所当然地认为光线是恒定的，但回想起来当然不是恒定的。

transcript.sec: Book Recommendation: Thinking Fast and Slow

turns.en: Do you have any other books that you recommend often or have reread yourself?
turns.zh: 你还有没有其他经常推荐或自己重读的书？

turns.en: So yeah, there's one I think I always go back to because it makes me think about the ideas in the book, but also it makes me think about my life, which is Thinking Fast and Slow by Daniel Kahneman. He's a Nobel laureate, amazing guy. When you read that, you really get to understand how your brain works, and then you can relate it to yourself and how you make decisions. It's about depressing the emotional part of your body and your decision making for the more logical, rational part. But then I also go, well, that's good for some decisions but not all decisions, because he really is advocating for getting rid of one system, the emotional system. And I like it because I can really choose which one I want to use, because sometimes there are fact-based decisions, and other times there are opinion-based decisions or emotional-based decisions, and you have to really understand which type of decision you're making for that given point in time.
turns.zh: 是的，有一本书我总会回头重读，因为它不仅让我思考书中的观点，也让我反思自己的生活，那就是丹尼尔·卡尼曼的《思考，快与慢》。他是诺贝尔奖得主，非常了不起。读这本书时，你会真正理解大脑是如何运作的，然后可以联系到自己以及自己如何做决策。这本书讲的是抑制你身体中情绪化的部分，让更逻辑、更理性的部分来做决策。但我也觉得，这对某些决策是好的，但不是所有决策都适用，因为他实际上是在倡导摒弃情绪系统。我喜欢这本书，是因为我可以真正选择使用哪个系统，因为有时是事实驱动的决策，有时是观点驱动的决策或情绪驱动的决策，你必须真正理解在特定时间点你正在做哪种类型的决策。

turns.en: Thinking Fast and Slow — I was, believe it or not, a subject as an undergrad in some of Danny Kahneman's experiments. You got paid, whatever, four dollars an hour to press spacebar for various experiments on that thinking fast, thinking slow separation.
turns.zh: 《思考，快与慢》——信不信由你，我本科时曾是丹尼·卡尼曼一些实验的受试者。我们每小时赚四美元，按空格键参与各种关于快思考和慢思考分离的实验。

turns.en: If we look at the emotion-based decisions, whether that's what I don't want to say impulsive but very quick decisions that are sort of made with a few million years of evolutionary input, like a pro/con list, or whether to retweet something very quickly without reading it — are there any examples that come to mind from your life where you've made very, very good snap decisions, where if you had tried to analyze that you would have made the wrong decision?
turns.zh: 如果我们看情绪驱动的决策——我不想说冲动，但就是那种非常快速、带着几百万年进化输入做出的决定，比如列利弊清单，或者没读就快速转发某条推文——你生活中有没有一些例子，你做了非常好的快速决定，而如果当时试图分析，反而会做出错误决定？

turns.en: Yeah, well, one of the best decisions I ever made in my life was to ask my wife to marry me, and I didn't know literally 15 minutes, 10 minutes before I was going to do it that I did it. Wow. I didn't even know, I had no clue. It was an amazing week, and that was after 11 weeks of dating. Then literally it was in a span of 15 minutes from the time I said I'm gonna do it, 10 minutes from the time I said I'm gonna do it till I did it. I wasn't prepared, I didn't even know that I wasn't prepared for her to say no.
turns.zh: 是的，我一生中最好的决定之一就是向我妻子求婚，而我在做之前 15 分钟、10 分钟都还不知道。哇，我完全不知道，毫无预兆。那是一个神奇的星期，那时我们才约会了 11 周。然后从我说“我要做”到真正去做，只有 15 分钟，从我说“我要做”到做完只有 10 分钟。我毫无准备，甚至都没想过她会说“不”该怎么办。

turns.en: Walk me through this a bit. What happened? I mean, did it just randomly pop into your head, or was there more to it on that day?
turns.zh: 跟我详细说说吧。发生了什么？是突然冒出来的念头，还是那天有更多铺垫？

turns.en: Well, yeah, there was. It was actually a Monday morning, of all things, real romantic, right? No. So what had happened was, I had some very, very close friends, and they came down. They said, 'Oh, I met this wonderful woman, and I would love to get you guys to meet her, and just see if we can all hang out.' Because the worst thing that ever happens — I don't know if you've had any friends like this — is you have a girl or a guy friend, and they're really great friends of yours, and then they meet somebody and they fall in love, and then all of a sudden you try to meet that person that they love so much, and you're like, 'I just don't click with them.' It's like, 'Oh, I almost lost a friend.' It's heart-wrenching. So I wanted my friends to like her, because I was 11 weeks in and we're having a great time, and I'm like, 'Am I in some crazy fog?' I was older at the time, so I had a lot of experience, and they saw some of my other relationships and gave me feedback on them. So they came over, and we hung out the whole weekend together, and we had an amazing time. So that morning, I was driving them to the airport after they said goodbye to my wife — then girlfriend — and all night long it was pouring rain, crazy rain. In the car, Sandy and Daniel, my friends, said, 'Oh my god, she's amazing. You guys are so in love. She's head and shoulders over any woman you've ever dated before.' And I was like, 'Yeah, that's how I feel.' I was on cloud nine. So I dropped them off, and then I turned around to come back because I was going to pick up my wife — then girlfriend — to drive to work together. As I'm coming back, the clouds open up, a rainbow opens up, and literally this brilliant sky comes into view. I'm thinking the whole time, 'Well, when's the right time? How do you know it's the right time that this is the right person?' Given my 15 years of dating history at the time, I'm like, 'It's never felt like this before.' I can work through all the rational arguments, but at some point you're like, 'Screw that.' So I'm driving home, and then all of a sudden one of my favorite songs comes on the radio. I'm like, 'Oh my god, this is amazing.' The rainbow and everything else. I drive up, and I had a rosebush in the front of the house. They're great roses often, but it was raining so hard that night. I looked at the rose, and I always clipped one and gave it to her. I looked at that and I was like, 'That survived tonight. It was the most beautiful rose I've ever seen.' I took off the thorns, clipped it, and I was going to just give it to her. So I walk in the door, and she's coming down the stairs with a basket of laundry — literally a basket of my laundry from like two weeks. She's coming down, and I'm looking at her. I go, 'Here's a rose, honey.' And she said, 'Oh, that's very nice.' And I said, 'Wait, no, no. Give me the basket, give me the rose.' I got down on one knee while she's on the stairs, and I said, 'Will you marry me?' Literally like that. I was blind. I just said, 'Will you marry me?' right there and then. That was a Monday morning. I had no ring, nothing.
turns.zh: 嗯，是的。那其实是个周一早上，最不浪漫的时候，对吧？不。事情是这样的，我有一些非常亲密的朋友，他们过来玩。他们说：“哦，我遇到了一个很棒的女人，我想让你们见见她，大家一起聚聚。”因为最糟糕的事情——不知道你有没有这样的朋友——就是你有一个很好的异性朋友，然后他们遇到了某人，坠入爱河，然后你试着去见那个他们深爱的人，结果发现“我就是和他们合不来”。你会想，“哦，我差点失去一个朋友。”这很让人揪心。所以我希望我的朋友们喜欢她，因为我们已经约会了 11 周，相处得很愉快，但我在想，“我是不是陷入了一种疯狂的迷雾？”我当时年纪大一些，有很多经验，他们见证过我之前的几段感情，也给过反馈。所以他们来了，我们一起度过了整个周末，非常开心。那天早上，我开车送他们去机场，他们刚和我妻子——当时还是女友——道别。整晚都在下倾盆大雨。在车里，我的朋友桑迪和丹尼尔说：“天哪，她太棒了。你们太相爱了。她比你之前约会过的任何女人都强出一大截。”我说：“是啊，我就是这么感觉的。”我高兴极了。我送他们下车，然后掉头回去接我妻子——当时还是女友——一起开车上班。回来的路上，云层散开，出现了一道彩虹，天空变得格外明亮。我一直在想：“什么时候才是合适的时机？你怎么知道这就是对的人？”考虑到我当时 15 年的约会史，我想：“以前从来没有这种感觉。”我可以列出所有理性的论据，但到了某个时刻，你会想：“去他的吧。”我开车回家，突然收音机里放了我最喜欢的一首歌。我想：“天哪，这太棒了。”还有彩虹什么的。我开到门口，房子前面有一丛玫瑰。平时花开得很好，但那天晚上雨下得很大。我看着那朵玫瑰——我经常剪一朵送给她。我想：“它今晚挺过来了。这是我见过最美的玫瑰。”我摘掉刺，剪下来，打算直接送给她。我走进门，她正提着一篮衣服下楼——真的是一篮我两周没洗的衣服。她走下来，我看着她。我说：“给你一朵玫瑰，亲爱的。”她说：“哦，真好看。”我说：“等等，不，不。把篮子给我，把玫瑰给我。”我单膝跪地，她站在楼梯上，我说：“你愿意嫁给我吗？”就这样。我完全是盲目的。我就在那里说了“你愿意嫁给我吗？”那是一个周一早上。我没有戒指，什么都没有。

turns.en: Wow, that's an incredible story. That is just incredible. Well, that's the kind of craziness — you know when you know. And the same thing happened in different parts of my life, like taking the job at Apple was just like that as well. It's crazy, crazy when you know you know. Amazing, Tony.
turns.zh: 哇，这故事太不可思议了。真是难以置信。这就是那种疯狂——你知道就是知道。同样的事情在我生活的其他方面也发生过，比如接受苹果的工作也是那样。当你知道了，你就知道了，这太疯狂了。太棒了，托尼。

turns.en: Let you get back to your day, but this has been so fun. I really appreciate you taking the time, and I certainly hope it's not the last time that we get to have a conversation. Perhaps we'll meet sometime in the future.
turns.zh: 让你回去忙吧，但这真的很有趣。非常感谢你抽出时间，我当然希望这不是我们最后一次交谈。也许我们将来会见面。

turns.en: Sure, anywhere. Let you know I'm in different places of the world. I'll be sure to stay in contact. And no thanks, this has been a lot of fun, and we went into all kinds of interesting questions that no one ever asked. So thank you.
turns.zh: 当然，任何地方都可以。告诉你，我世界各地到处跑。我一定会保持联系。不用谢，这真的很有趣，我们聊了很多别人从未问过的有趣问题。所以谢谢你。

turns.en: Oh, my pleasure. And if people can find Future Shape at future-shape.com? Is that the right URL?
turns.zh: 哦，这是我的荣幸。如果人们想找到 Future Shape，是 future-shape.com 吗？网址对吗？

turns.en: That's correct. And what is your best social? On Twitter, is that the best? People at tfadell, T-F-A-D-E-L-L.
turns.zh: 没错。你最好的社交平台是 Twitter 吗？是 @tfadell，T-F-A-D-E-L-L。

turns.en: And I'll link to all of that and everything else we discussed in the show notes for people, so they'll be able to find you very easily. Is there anything that you'd like to say, recommend, conclude with, suggest any parting comments or anything at all before we wrap up?
turns.zh: 我会把所有内容以及我们讨论的一切都链接到节目笔记里，这样大家就能很容易找到你。在我们结束之前，你还有什么想说的、推荐的、总结的，或者任何临别赠言吗？

turns.en: You know, I think the biggest thing is that we all go through all of these changes in our lives. We go through failure. If you are just open to learning and you're open to the failure and pushing hard... You know, I didn't have the best start. I tried really hard, but I didn't have the best start. But it can end up happening if you really keep pushing, whether it's a failure or a success. Keep pushing even beyond success, and keep staying a beginner, trying to stay humble, and trying to work with others, because that's your superpower at the end of the day.
turns.zh: 你知道吗，我认为最重要的一点是，我们一生中都会经历所有这些变化。我们会经历失败。只要你愿意学习，愿意接受失败并努力拼搏……我一开始并不顺利。我非常努力，但起步并不好。但如果你真的不断努力，无论是失败还是成功，最终都能有所成就。即使成功之后也要继续努力，保持初学者的心态，保持谦逊，并尝试与他人合作，因为归根结底，这才是你的超能力。

turns.en: Yeah, for sure. And you know, in your TED talk, you talked about seeing the invisible problems instead of just the obvious problems. And it strikes me that you've done that looking out at the world, but also looking inward at your own subjective experience, as in working with that psychologist to become more aware of your internal processes. And it's just such an incredible and inspiring story. I mean, for the purposes of helping people, I think it's so wonderful. I'm sure it was difficult at the time that you had so many challenges at various points in the road.
turns.zh: 是的，确实如此。你知道吗，在你的 TED 演讲中，你谈到了要看到那些看不见的问题，而不仅仅是显而易见的问题。我觉得你不仅向外观察世界，也向内审视自己的主观体验，比如和那位心理学家合作，让自己更了解内心的过程。这真是一个令人难以置信且鼓舞人心的故事。我是说，为了帮助他人，我觉得这非常棒。我相信当时一定很艰难，你在人生的不同阶段遇到了很多挑战。

turns.en: Yeah, absolutely. There was, and there's always challenges. And I'm still learning and I'm still growing and all those things. But you're gonna go through them too. Everyone's got to go through them. And just embrace them. And just because you see success doesn't mean they didn't go through them as well. They most likely did, or if they haven't, they will at some point. Everyone does.
turns.zh: 是的，绝对如此。挑战一直存在，而且永远会有。我仍在学习，仍在成长，诸如此类。但你也一样会经历这些。每个人都要经历。拥抱它们吧。你看到别人成功，并不意味着他们没有经历过这些。他们很可能经历过，或者如果没有，将来某个时候也会经历。每个人都会。

turns.en: Oh, for sure. Yeah, you should keep going. Everyone's fighting or has fought battles that you know nothing about. Everybody's got their own stuff. Thank you so much, Tony.
turns.zh: 哦，当然。是的，你应该继续前进。每个人都在战斗，或者曾经战斗过，而你对此一无所知。每个人都有自己的事情。非常感谢你，Tony。

turns.en: Good, thanks Jim. And have a great day. Looking forward to staying in touch.
turns.zh: 好的，谢谢 Jim。祝你有美好的一天。期待保持联系。

turns.en: Likewise. All right, my man. Cheers, man. Bye bye.
turns.zh: 彼此彼此。好的，老兄。干杯，再见。
